content:
NCCN Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors Panel Members Summary of the Guidelines Updates 

- Clinical Presentations and Diagnosis (CP 1) N ETs of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2), Lung, and Thymus (NET-1) NETs of the Pancreas (Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2) (PanNET-1) SSTR-Negative Disease (SSTRNEG-1) Well-Diferentiated, Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors (WDG3-1) - Neuroendocrine Neoplasms of Unknown Primary (NUP 1) Extrapulmonary Poorly Diferentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (PDNEC-1) - Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma (PHEO 1) - Adrenal Gland Tumors (AGT 1) - Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia, Type 1 (MEN1 1) - Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia, Type 2 (MEN2 1) 

- Principles of Biochemical Testing (NE A) - Principles of Imaging (NE B) - Principles of Endoscopic Treatment of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE C) - Principles of Surgical Management of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE D) - Principles of Pathology (NE E) - Principles of Hereditary Cancer Risk Assessment and Genetic Counseling (NE F) - - Principles of Systemic Anti Tumor Therapy (NE G) - - Principles of Liver Directed Therapy for Neuroendocrine Tumor Metastases (NE H) - Principles of Radiation Therapy (NE I) - Principles of Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy with Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate (NE J) - Principles of Hormone Control (NE K) - Staging (ST 1) 

Find an NCCN Member Institution: - https://www.nccn.org/home/member institutions. 

**NCCN Categories of Evidence and Consensus:** All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated. 

See NCCN Categories of Evidence and Consensus. 

**NCCN Categories of Preference:** All recommendations are considered appropriate. See NCCN Categories of Preference. 

- Abbreviations (ABBR 1) 

The NCCN Guidelines[®] are a statement of evidence and consensus of the authors regarding their views of currently accepted approaches to treatment. Any clinician seeking to apply or consult the NCCN Guidelines is expected to use independent medical judgment in the context of individual clinical circumstances to determine any patient’s care or treatment. The National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ) makes no representations or warranties of any kind regarding their content, use or application and disclaims any responsibility for their application or use in any way. The NCCN Guidelines are copyrighted by National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] . All rights reserved. header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- Footnote revised: Principles of Pathology ~~for the Diagnosis of Neuroendocrine Neoplasms (~~ NE-E). 

- Principles of Endoscopic Treatment of NeuroendocrineTumors (NE-C) link added where appropriate. 

- Liquid biopsy changed to circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) testing. 

- Hyperlinks to appropriate Principles pages added where indicated. 

- Principles of Systemic Anti-Tumor Therapy table headers revised with addition of: (alphabetical order by category, order does not imply preference)

- Neuroendocrine Tumors (NETs) of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2), Lung, and Thymus, new bullet added: Multiple lung nodules or tumorlets and evidence of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia (DIPNECH) (NET-13) 

- New header added: Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs (SSTRNEG-1) 

- New footnote b added: Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A). 

Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2), Lung and Thymus 

NET-1 

- Following Hyper-gastrinemic/Type 1, First-Line Treatment, options revised: 

- �Endoscopic ~~resection of >1 cm gastric tumors~~ _treatment_ (NE-C) ~~→ EGD at 1 y and then every 1–3 y thereafter~~ 

- �Removed: Metastatic disease (NET-9) 

- Following Hper-gastrinemic/Type 2: 

- �New column added: 

   - Multiphasic abdomen CT or MRI (NE-B) 

   - SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI (NE-B) 

- �First-Line Treatment, option revised: Endoscopic ~~resection of >1 cm gastric tumors~~ _treatment_ (NE-C) and _multidisciplinary approach to treat the underlying gastrinoma (_ See ~~gastinoma (P~~ anNET-3 ~~)~~ for identification and treatment of the gastrin-producing tumor _)_ 

- Following Normal gastrin/Type 3: 

- �First bullet revised: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) _to determine depth of invasion and assess for lymphadenopathy_ 

- �First-Line Treatment, bottom option revised: . . .or _If no evidence of regional lymphadenopathy on EUS or other imaging_ , consider endoscopic _resection (NE-C)_ or surgical wedge resection (NE-D) → _Surveillance_ (NET-8) 

- Following evaluation, bottom pathway revised: ~~Other~~ ~~_P_~~ _PI-induced_ → _Endoscopic treatment (NE-C)_ 

NET-1A 

- Footnote b revised: ~~May need multiple biopsies throughout the entire stomach.~~ ~~_T_~~ _he minimum standard is to obtain at least two biopsies from the antrum (lesser and greater curvature), one biopsy from the incisura (angularis), and two biopsies from the gastric body (lesser and greater curvature). Dixon MF, et al. Am J Surg Pathol 1996;20:1161-1181._ 

NET-2 

- Surveillance, top option revised: Endoscopic surveillance ~~every 3–12 mo for the frst y and then annually thereafter~~ ~~_(_~~ _NE-C)_ 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**
content:
NET-3 

- Following Simple appendectomy, third pathway revised: . . .or Any tumor size with incomplete resection or positive _lymph_ nodes ~~/margins~~ _within the appendectomy specimen (if available for analysis) (NE-D)_ 

- Footnote p, link revised: See NCCN Guidelines for ~~Colon Cancer~~ ~~_A_~~ _ppendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers_ . 

- Footnote removed: Data are limited on survival benefit from right hemicolectomy. 

NET-4 

- Evaluation, As appropriate, bullet removed: Consider baseline 5-HIAA in asymptomatic patients with high burden of metastatic disease (NE-A) 

- Footnote u, new bullet added: Resection to root of mesentery. 

NET-5 

- Surveillance: 

- �Top option revised: ~~No additional follow-up required~~ ~~_E_~~ _ndoscopic surveillance (NE-C)_ 

- �Following T1, surveillance revised: 

   - Bullets removed: 

   - <1 cm: No follow-up required 

   - 1 to ≤2 cm: Endoscopy with rectal MRI or endorectal ultrasound at 6 and 12 mo, then as clinically indicated 

   - New option added: Endoscopic surveillance (NE-C) 

NET-6 

- Evaluation revised to: Consider _if clinically indicated_ NET-8 

- 12 wk **–** 12 mo post-resection: 

- �Bullets removed: 

   - Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI for primary GI NETs (as clinically indicated for lung and thymic NETs) (NE-B) (Also for >1 y post-resection to 10y:) 

   - Chest CT + CT or MRI abdomen with contrast for primary lung/thymic NETs (as clinically indicated for primary GI tumors) 

- �New bullets added: (Also for >1 y post-resection to 10y:) 

   - Primary GI NETs 

   - Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI (NE-B) 

   - Chest CT ± contrast (as clinically indicated) 

   - Primary lung/thymic NETs 

   - Chest CT + multiphasic CT or MRI abdomen with contrast 

   - Pelvis CT or MRI with contrast (as clinically indicated) (NE-B) 

- >1 y post-resection to 10y: 

- �Bullet removed: Chest/abdomen CT with contrast for primary lung/thymic NETs (as clinically indicated for primary GI tumors) 

- NET-9 

- Evaluation, fourth bullet revised: _Routine_ ~~B~~ ~~_b_~~ iochemical evaluation ~~as clinically indicated~~ ~~_w_~~ _ith 5-HIAA (for ileal, jejunal, and cecal NETs) in patients with symptoms and consider in asymptomatic patients with metastatic disease_ (NE-A) 

NET-10 

- Subsequent Therapy, Locoregional therapy options, first bullet revised: _Consider_ liver-directed therapy for liver-predominant disease (NE-H) NET-11 

- New footnote added: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**
Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Pancreas (Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2)

content:
- Treatment column following >1 to ≤2cm revised: Observation ~~in select cases o~~ r Pancreatectomy ~~or Enucleation in select cases (~~ NE-D) 

- Footnote i revised: As appropriate, central pancreatectomy or spleen-preserving surgery ~~should~~ ~~_c_~~ _an_ be considered. PanNET-4 

- Following Resectable: 

- �Options removed: 

   - Duodenum → Duodenotomy and intraoperative ultrasound; local resection/ enucleation of tumor(s) + periduodenal node dissection (NE-D) 

   - Head → Pancreatoduodenectomy (NE-D) 

   - Distal → Distal pancreatectomy + splenectomy + regional nodes (NE-D) 

- �New option added: Primary located → Resection 

- Footnote removed: Preoperative vaccination against encapsulated bacterium (ie, pneumococcus, haemophilus influenzae b, meningococcal group C), if considering surgery with possible splenectomy. See Principles of Surgical Management of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE-D 2 of 3). 

- PanNET-6 

- Footnote q added: In highly-selected cases where the lesion is resectable but the patient is not a surgical candidate, EUS-guided ablation can be considered at a center with expertise.

- Footnote removed: Small (<2 cm), peripheral glucagonomas are rare; enucleation/local excision + peripancreatic lymph dissection may be considered. PanNET-10 

- Footnote removed: Small (<2 cm), peripheral VIPomas are rare; enucleation/local excision + peripancreatic lymph dissection may be considered. Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2 NETs
- New SSTR-Negative Disease recommendations added.

- Treatment following Unfavorable biology, options revised: . . .Resection with regional lymphadenectomy ~~if feasible~~ ~~_i_~~ _f appropriate after multidisciplinary review_ , or Neoadjuvant ~~chemo~~ _systemic_ therapy on a case-by-case basis (eg, Ki-67 ≥55%). 

- Footnotes added: 

- �Footnote a: Principles of Pathology (NE-E). 

- �Footnote h: There is no role for adjuvant therapy outside of a clinical trial. Consider enrollment in a clinical trial. 

- WDG3-4 

- Treatment, new option added: Resection may play a role in select cases (NE-D). 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**
content:
Extrapulmonary Poorly Diferentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm 

PDNEC-1 

- Evaluation: 

- �Recommended: 

   - First bullet revised: Multiphasic chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (NE-B) ~~or~~ 

   - New bullet added: Molecular profiling of tumor tissue. 

- �As appropriate, bullet removed: Consider molecular profiling of tumor tissue. 

- Treatment, second column on bottom revised: If progression _:_ ~~(~~ NE-G ~~): Chemotherapy, Immunotherapy, Targeted therapy.~~ 

- Footnote g revised: Tumor/somatic molecular profiling ~~should be considered~~ _is recommended_ for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. ~~Consider specifcally testing for potentially actionable somatic fndings including, but not limited to: NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E mutations, microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H), mismatch repair defciency (dMMR), and tumor mutational burdenhigh (TMB-H). T~~ esting on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ~~liquid biopsy~~ ~~_c_~~ _tDNA testing_ can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 

- Footnote removed: Pembrolizumab can be considered for patients with MSI-H, dMMR, or advanced TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors (as determined by an FDA-approved test) that have progressed following prior treatment and have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.

PHEO-1 

• Evaluation, Recommended, first bullet revised: Plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated _total_ metanephrines and ~~normetanephrines~~ _catecholamines_ (NEA 2 of 4). PHEO-3 

- Surveillance: 

- �Resectable disease: 

   - 12 wk **–** 12 mo post-resection, first bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated _total_ metanephrines and ~~normetanephrines~~ _catecholamines_ (NE-A 2 of 4) 

   - >1 y post _-curative_ resection ~~up to 10y:~~ 

   - First bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and markers _annually or sooner if patient is symptomatic_ (NE-A 2 of 4) 

   - Sub-bullets removed: 

   - Years 1–3: every 6–12 mo 

   - Years 4+ up to 10 y: annually 

   - Header removed: >10 y: 

   - Bullet removed: Consider surveillance as clinically indicated 

- �Locally unresectable disease or Distant metastases, first bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated _total_ metanephrines and ~~normetanephrines~~ _catecholamines_ (NE-A 2 of 4)

- _washout_ 

- • Following Morphologic evaluation, Adrenal protocol CT scan, first bullet, first sub-bullet revised: If Hounsfield unit (HU) < +10, no further imaging _needed on this scan_ 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- Following Primary aldosteronism, top option revised: Not a surgical candidate _or surgery not desired per patient_ AGT-3 

- Additional Evaluation, Adrenal protocol CT scan, first sub-bullet, first tertiary-bullet revised: If Hounsfield unit (HU) < +10, no further _washout_ imaging 

- _needed on this scan_ 

- First-Line Treatment, bottom option revised: ~~Open~~ _A_ drenalectomy f ~~or suspected malignancy~~ ~~_(_~~ _NE-D)_ AGT-4 

- First-Line Treatment, top option revised: ~~Open~~ _A_ drenalectomy ~~for suspected carcinoma~~ _(NE-D)_ 

AGT-5 

- Treatment following Localized disease, second column, third bullet revised: Consider adjuvant Mitotane therapy ~~(category 3)~~ 

- Follow-Up interval for both pathways revised: Every 12 wk **–** 12 mo up to ~~5~~ ~~_1_~~ _0_ y (after ~~5~~ ~~_1_~~ _0_ y as clinically indicated)

- Page header revised: MEN1 ~~Surveillance~~ _Screening_ 

- Parathyroid: 

- �Bullet revised: Calcium _and albumin_ annually 

- �Second column, new bullet added: Alkaline phosphatase 

- PanNET, second bullet revised: Consider abdomen/pelvis _MRI (preferred) or_ CT ~~or MRI~~ with contrast every 1–3 y 

- Pituitary, first bullet revised: Pituitary ~~or sella~~ MRI with contrast ~~of pituitary~~ every 3–5 y 

- New header added: Other neoplasia 

- �New bullets added: 

   - Angiomas 

   - Adrenal tumors 

   - Collagenomas 

   - Lipomas 

   - Angiofibromas 

• New footnote c added: Further studies should be done, especially in patients with suspected MEN1, when the serum calcium is ≥10 mg/dL since normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism is noted especially as an early sign of the disease. Hyperparathyroidism evaluations should include consideration of other secondary causes of hypercalcemia. 

MEN1-2 

- Far left column, new bullet added: Other tumors may be associated with MEN 

- Clinical Evaluation: 

- Parathyroid, first bullet, new sub-bullet added: Consider bone mineral density (if age appropriate) 

- �Pituitary: 

   - First sub-bullet revised: Pituitary ~~or sella~~ MRI with contrast 

   - New sub-bullet added: Consider referral to endocrinology and neurosurgery for further workup 

- Treatment, following Pituitary, option revised: ~~Consider referral to endocrinology for further workup~~ _Medical versus surgical treatment based on hormone function and/or tumor size_ 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**
content:
MEN1-2 (continued) 

- New footnotes added: 

- �Footnote e: Clinical presentation dictates the most appropriate biochemical and radiologic investigation 

- �Footnote i: Studies showed better prognosis when thymectomy is done at same time as partial parathyroidectomy. 

- Footnote j: Parathyroid surgery will likely differ for spontaneous hyperparathyroidism. 

- �Footnote k: For surgical approach, recommend evaluation by high-volume parathyroid surgeon. 

MEN1-3 

- Bullet removed: Endoscopy with EUS is recommended prior to pancreatic surgery in order to preoperatively assess and localize tumors. MEN1-4 

- New references added: 

- �Reference 1: Brandi ML, et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2025;13:699-721. Published correction appears in Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2025;13:e14. 

- �Reference 8: Toraih EA, AbdAlnaeem MA, Bobba T, et al. The role of thymectomy during parathyroidectomy in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1-associated hyperparathyroidism: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Surg Oncol 2025;23:272. 

- References removed: 

- �Yates CJ, Newey PJ, Thakker RV. Challenges and controversies in management of pancreatic neuroendocrine tumours in patients with MEN1. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2015;3:895-905. 

- �Frost M, Lines KE, Thakker RV. Current and emerging therapies for PNETs in patients with or without MEN1. Nat Rev Endocrinol 2018;14:216-227. 

- NE-A 1 of 4 

- Carcinoid syndrome (NETs of GI tract), Location revised: Primary tumors in small bowel and ~~appendix~~ _cecum_ ; rarely in rectum NE-A 2 of 4 

- PCC/PGL: 

- �Clinical Signs or Symptoms revised: . . .sweating, _paroxysmal headache, pallor episodes,_ syncope _(very rare)_ 

- �Testing, first bullet revised: Plasma free or 24-hour urine fractionated _total_ metanephrines and ~~normetanephrines~~ _catecholamines_ 

- Pituitary tumor, Testing: 

- �Fourth bullet revised: Alpha subunits _(mainly in patients with non-secretory tumors)_ 

- �Sixth bullet revised: Plasma adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)/ ~~random~~ ~~_A_~~ _M_ cortisol 

- Hypercortisolemia, Clinical Signs or Symptoms revised: Cushing syndrome (central weight gain, striae, hypertension, hyperglycemia, depression, hirsutism _, muscle weakness_ ) 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- Page header revised: Principles of Imaging _for Neuroendocrine Neoplasms_ 

- Anatomic Imaging: 

- �Second bullet revised: . . .When possible, SSTR-PET/CT and SSTR-PET/MRI should be performed concurrently with diagnostic multiphase CT or MRI. _Consider same imaging modality and contrast agent (either multiphasic CT or MRI) for follow-up studies for consistent imaging interpretation._ 

- �Third bullet revised: Without a known tumor or specific clinical concern, imaging should include chest CT ± contrast _per institutional standards_ and imaging of the brain is generally not required for well-differentiated NET. 

- Functional Imaging: 

- First bullet, new sub-bullet added: For qualitative lesion assessment, Modified Krenning score can be used. Krenning score is based on the lesion with the highest SSTR uptake: 

   - 0, no uptake 

   - 1, very low uptake 

   - 2, uptake ≤ liver 

   - 3, uptake > liver 

   - 4, uptake > spleen 

- �Last bullet revised: Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET, even for lower grade tumors, and a combined score of FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives more prognostic value ~~and~~ _,_ outperforms pathologic grading _, and may identify more active disease_ . 

- NE-B 3 of 5 

- New Principles of Imaging for Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma added. NE-B 4 of 5 

- New Principles of Imaging for Adrenocortical Carcinoma added. 

NE-B 5 of 5 

- New reference 2 added: Chan DL, Hayes AR, Karfis I, et al. Dual [[68] Ga]DOTATATE and [[18] F]FDG PET/CT in patients with metastatic gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine neoplasms: a multicentre validation of the NETPET score. Br J Cancer 2023;128:549-555. 

- NE-C 

- New Principles of Endoscopic Treatment of Neuroendocrine Tumors added. NE-D 1 of 3 

- New bullets added: 

- �Second bullet: Gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs may be endoscopically resectable, see NE-I. 

- �Fourth bullet, second sub-bullet, new tertiary-bullet added: Grade 1 PanNETs <2 cm can be safely observed and is the recommended treatment course. Grade 2 PanNETs should be considered for surgical resection if patient factors allow. 

- �Fifth bullet: After simple appendectomy, upon examination of the specimen, if >2 cm and positive lymph nodes in the specimen, recommendation is complete staging imaging and proceed to right hemicolectomy. Margin positivity can be considered a high-risk feature based on size and discussion with patient for complete right hemicolectomy. 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- �Third bullet: Resection of GI NETs should include adequate regional lymph node resection (including all palpable disease where feasible). ~~This is especially important for small bowel NETs were manual palpation of the entire length of bowel is recommended as the rate of synchronous primary tumors is high (15%–30% incidence)~~ _Palpate the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve_ . . . 

- �Sixth bullet: . . .Tumors of the head are generally treated with pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure); tumors of the body and tail are treated with distal pancreatectomy ~~and~~ _with or without_ splenectomy or spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. . . 

NE-D 2 of 3

- �Second bullet: Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve progression-free survival ~~for patients with functioning tumors.~~ . . 

- �Sixth bullet: ~~Octreotide therapy should be considered parenterally prior to induction of anesthesia in patients with carcinoid syndrome to prevent carcinoid crisis.~~ ~~_P_~~ _rophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide administration should_ . 

- _not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration to treat carcinoid crisis_ 

- New bullet added: For patients with clinical stage I or II ACC (or suspected ACC), achieving R0 resection is of critical importance. It is recommended that these operations be performed by surgeons with high volume experience in adrenal surgery. Although an open approach is typically advised, choosing a minimally invasive technique should rely on the surgeon’s experience. 

- NE-D 3 of 3

- �Reference 4: Mayo SC, de Jong MC, Pulitano C, et al. Surgical management of hepatic neuroendocrine tumor metastasis: Results from an international multi-institutional analysis. Ann Surg Oncol 2010;17:3129-3136. 

- �Reference 5: Gudmundsdottir H, Habermann EB, Vierkant RA, et al. Survival and symptomatic relief after cytoreductive hepatectomy for neuroendocrine tumor liver metastases: Long-term follow-up evaluation of more than 500 patients. Ann Surg Oncol 2023;30:4840-4851. 

- �Reference 6: Yip L, Duh QY, Wachtel H, et al. American Association of Endocrine Surgeons Guidelines for Adrenalectomy: Executive Summary. JAMA Surg 2022;157:870-877. 

- Reference removed: Maxwell JE, Naraev B, Halperin DM, Choti MA, Halfdanarson TR. Shifting paradigms in the pathophysiology and treatment of carcinoid crisis. Ann Surg Oncol 2022;29:3072-3084. 

NE-E 1 of 9 

- Required Information, first bullet, third sub-bullet revised: In cases of mixed neuroendocrine neoplasms (with the smaller tumor component at least 30% of the total tumor mass), each component must be diagnosed and graded based on ~~AJCC~~ ~~_W_~~ _HO_ tumor nomenclature and following the criteria of tumor differentiation. 

**Continued**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**
content:
NE-F 1 of 6 

- Content removed: 

- �General bullets 

- �1) Pre-Test Counseling bullets 

- �2) Considerations When Determining the Most Appropriate Testing Strategy bullets 

- �3) Post-Test Counseling Includes the Following Elements bullets 

- New bullets added: 

- �For detailed discussion of Principles of Cancer Risk Assessment and Genetic Counseling, see NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate (EVAL-A, EVAL-B). 

- �For patients with a clinical diagnosis of an endocrine neoplasia condition (such as MEN1) and negative genetic testing, consider following the related 

   - surveillance recommendations for patient and first-degree family members. 

- �For patients with positive results: For many hereditary endocrine neoplasia syndromes, testing of children is indicated since screening interventions often start in childhood or adolescence. 

- Recommend Genetic Risk Evaluation and Genetic Testing for Hereditary Endocrine Neoplasia Syndromes for Patients Meeting Any of the Following Criteria: 

- �Bullets revised: 

   - Eight bullet: A mutation identified on tumor genomic testing that has clinical implications if also identified in the germline ~~(eg, tumor analysis shows mutation in~~ ~~_BRCA1/2_ or MMR gene)~~ 

   - Ninth bullet: ~~Close~~ _Any_ blood relative with a known pathogenic variant/likely pathogenic variant in a cancer susceptibility gene 

- �New bullets added: 

   - Gastrinoma (duodenal/pancreatic or type 2 gNET) 

   - Multifocal PanNETs 

- �Sixth bullet, new sub-bullet added: Other tumors reported in association with MEN1 

- Consider Genetic Risk Evaluation and Genetic Testing for Patients Meeting Any of the Following Criteria: 

- �New bullets added: 

   - Thymic NET at any age 

   - Patients <30 years with apparently sporadic, functioning pituitary adenoma or a nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma >1 cm 

   - Children and adolescents with an apparently sporadic pituitary adenoma 

- �Bullets removed: 

   - Gastrinoma (duodenal/pancreatic or type 2 gNET) 

   - Multifocal PanNETs 

NE-F 2 of 6 

- Note at top of page revised: This resource is not intended to be an exhaustive list of hereditary endocrine neoplasias. Specific scenarios may warrant consideration of less common conditions such as Carney complex, Carney triad, _CDC73-related disorders (hyperparathyroidism),_ Currarino syndrome, or polycythemia-paraganglioma-somatostatinoma syndrome. (Also page NE-F 3 of 6) 

**Continued**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- New reference 1 added: Brandi ML, Pieterman CRC, English KA, et al. Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1): Recommendations and guidelines for best practice. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2025;13:699-721. Published correction in Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2025;13:e14. 

- NE-G 1 of 11 

- Useful in Certain Circumstances, third bullet revised: Consider ~~(listed in alphabetical order): Cytotoxic~~ ~~_c_~~ _ytotoxic_ chemotherapy, if no other options feasible ~~(all category 3):~~ 

- �Tertiary bullet revised: Oxaliplatin _-based therapy (eg, CAPEOX [Capecitabine/Oxaliplatin] or FOLFOX [Leucovorin/Fluorouracil/Oxaliplatin])_ �Tertiary bullet removed: Dacarbazine 

- Footnote g revised: The PROMID trial showed an ~~antitumor~~ _antiproliferative_ effect of octreotide acetate LAR in advanced NETs of the midgut. The CLARINET trial showed an ~~antitumor~~ _antiproliferative_ effect of lanreotide in advanced, well-differentiated metastatic grade 1 and grade 2 GEP NETs. (Also page NE-G 3 of 11) 

- New footnotes added: 

- Footnote d: Randomized data suggest superior outcomes in terms of PFS and response rates with PRRT compared to small molecules. Significant impact on overall survival is lacking. (Also page NE-G 3 of 11) 

- Footnote j: For information regarding _DPYD_ testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. (Also pages NE-G 2 of 11 through NE-G 6 of 11) NE-G 2 of 11 

- Other Recommended, new options added: 

- �Oxaliplatin-based therapy (eg, CAPEOX or FOLFOX) 

- �Moved from Useful in Certain Circumstances: 

   - Carboplatin/Etoposide 

   - Capecitabine/Temozolomide 

   - Cisplatin/Etoposide 

   - Temozolomide 

- Useful in Certain Circumstances, option removed: If progression on standard SSA doses, above-label dose Octreotide acetate LAR or Lanreotide (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms [category 2B]) 

- Footnote removed: After clinical, symptomatic, or radiographic progression on standard SSA doses, for symptom and/or tumor control, above-label dosing octreotide acetate LAR (up to 60 mg once a month) or lanreotide (up to 120 mg every 14 days) may be useful in select cases. 

- NE-G 4 of 11 

- Well-Differentiated, Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors, Favorable Biology, preference stratification applied to all options. 

- New footnote r added: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab. (Also pages NE-G 5 of 11, NE-G 6 of 11, and NE-G 8 of 11) 

**Continued**

**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- Well-Differentiated, Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors, Unfavorable Biology, preference stratification applied to all options. �Locally Advanced/Metastatic Disease: 

   - Preferred, last option revised: Oxaliplatin-based therapy ( ~~ie~~ ~~_e_~~ _g,_ CAPEOX, _FOLFIRINOX [Leucovorin/Fluorouracil/Irinotecan/Oxaliplatin],_ or FOLFOX) 

   - Other Recommended, second option revised: Irinotecan-based therapy (eg, Cisplatin/Irinotecan, FOLFIRI [Leucovorin/Fluorouracil/Irinotecan] ~~, or FOLFIRINOX)~~ 

- New footnote s added: For information regarding _UGT1A1_ testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. (Also page NE-G 6 of 11) NE-G 6 of 11 

- Extrapulmonary Poorly Differentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm, preference stratification applied to all options. 

- NE-G 7 of 11 

- Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma, Locally Unresectable/Distant Metastases, preference stratification applied to all options. 

- Other Recommended, option removed: High-specific-activity (HSA) iobenguane I-131 or other 131I-MIBG (requires prior positive MIBG scan) 

- �Useful in Certain Circumstances, option revised: Selpercatinib _(if RET gene fusion-positive)_ 

- Footnote removed: HSA iobenguane I-131 is an FDA-approved option. 

- NE-G 9 of 11 

- New references added: 

- Reference 9: Capdevila J, Amthauer H, Ansquer C, et al. Efficacy, safety and subgroup analysis of 177Lu-edotreotide vs everolimus in patients with grade 1 or grade 2 GEP-NETs: Phase III COMPETE trial [abstract]. Ann Oncol 2025;36:Abstract 1706O. 

- Reference 10: Baudin E, Walter TA, Beron A, et al. First multicentric randomized phase II trial investigating the antitumor efficacy of peptide receptor radionucleide therapy with 177lutetium-octreotate (OCLU) in unresectable progressive neuroendocrine pancreatic tumor: Results of the OCLURANDOM trial [abstract]. Ann Oncol 2022;33:Abstract 887O. 

NE-G 10 of 11 

- Reference 42 revised: ~~Solomon B, Drilon A, Lin JJ, et al. Repotrectinib in patients (pts) with NTRK fusion-positive (NTRK+) advanced solid tumors, including NSCLC: Update from the phase I/II TRIDENT-1 trial [abstract]. Ann Oncol 2023;34:Abstract 1372P.~~ ~~_B_~~ _esse B, Lin JJ, Bazhenova L, et al. Repotrectinib in NTRK fusion-positive advanced solid tumors: A phase 1/2 trial. Nat Med 2026;32:682-689._ 

- New reference 44 added: Jimenez C, Andreassen M, Durand A, et al. Belzutifan for advanced pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma. N Engl J Med 2025;393:2012-2022. 

NE-J 1 of 3 

- Top of page, new bullet added: First-line PRRT (typically along with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide) can be considered in patients with advanced GEP-NETs with Ki-67 10%–55%. 

- Key Eligibility: 

- �Second bullet revised: SSTR expression ~~of~~ ~~_b_~~ _y the_ NET as detected by SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI _. The threshold for PRRT is tumor uptake greater than normal liver, see_ (NE-B). 

- Last bullet, new sub-bullet added: PRRT should generally be avoided in patients with glomerular filtration rate (GFR) <30, and used with caution in GFR 30–50. 

**Continued**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- Preparing Eligible Patients for Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate: 

- �New bullets added: 

   - Prior to PRRT, surgical evaluation should be performed. Resection of primary tumors may reduce the risk of PRRT-induced bowel obstruction or ischemia and decrease of overall tumor burden may improve PRRT responses. Additionally, liver cytoreduction post PRRT can be more technically challenging. 

   - Short-term toxicities: 

- �Third bullet revised: Counsel patients about the _long-term_ risks of: 

   - Second sub-bullet revised: Myelosuppression _, less commonly s_ econdary myelodysplastic syndrome and leukemia 

   - Fourth sub-bullet revised: Hepatic toxicity _(in patients with extremely high liver tumor burden or preexisting liver dysfunction)_ 

   - New sub-bullet added: Bowel obstruction and bowel ischemia (rare) in the setting of disease in the root of mesentery occluding mesenteric vessels or peritoneal/mesenteric carcinomatosis. Consider post-treatment prophylactic steroids in patients at risk. 

- �Bullet removed: Do not administer long-acting SSAs (eg, octreotide LAR, lanreotide) for 4 weeks prior to each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment. Administer short-acting octreotide as needed for symptom control of carcinoid syndrome; discontinue at least 24 hours prior to initiating lutetium Lu 177 dotatate. 

NE-J 2 of 3 

- Dose and Administration: 

- �First bullet revised: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate is administered via peripheral IV at a dose of 200 mCi over 30–40 minutes every 8 weeks for a total of 4 treatments (unless dose modification required secondary to adverse reactions). _Administration is by gravity drip or programmable infusion pump._ 

- �Second bullet, sub-bullets revised: 

   - Second sub-bullet: _In general, a_ mino acids are administered 30 minutes before, concurrently with, and 3 hours after lutetium Lu 177 dotatate. 

   - Third sub-bullet revised: Solutions containing only arginine/lysine are _preferred as they_ ~~only available through compounding pharmacies, but~~ are much less emetogenic than commercial amino acid solutions, _but these are only available through compounding pharmacies. Two_ ~~Options~~ _options_ for _renal protective_ ~~compounded a~~ mino acids _administration_ are as follows: 

   - First tertiary bullet revised: Arginine 2.5%/lysine 2.5% in 1000 mL NaCl infused at 250 mL/h for 4 hours. _Routine prophylactic antiemetics may not be necessary if selective (arginine/lysine) amino acid solutions are used, but should be available as needed._ 

   - Second tertiary bullet revised: Commercial amino acid formulation ( _nonselective,_ typically containing approximately 20 amino acids) mixed in sterile water for a total volume of approximately 2000 mL. _Aggressive antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended for patients when nonselective amino acid solutions are used._ Infusion rate _of the amino acids_ can be increased to roughly 300–500 mL/h, as tolerated. Recommend starting at low rate of 50 mL/h and increasing by 10 mL/h every 10 minutes as tolerated based on symptoms such as nausea. ~~Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate infusion should begin after at least 250 mL of amino acids have been infused.~~ 

   - Sub-bullet removed: Commercial (nonselective) amino acid formulations infused at high rates are more emetogenic than compounded amino acids. 

- �New bullet added: Although acute hormonal reactions are rare, SSA should be readily available for urgent management depending on the syndrome or the symptoms. 

- �Bullet removed: Antiemetic medications should be available prior to and during amino acid and lutetium Lu 177 dotatate infusions. Aggressive antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended for patients when nonselective amino acid solutions are used with PRRT. Routine prophylactic antiemetics may not be necessary if selective (arginine/lysine) amino acid solutions are used, but should be available as needed. 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:**

content:
- �Third bullet revised: SSAs (octreotide _acetate LAR_ or lanreotide) can be administered 4–24 hours after each ~~lutetium Lu 177 dotatate~~ ~~_P_~~ _RRT_ treatment. 

- �New bullet added: Starting within 24 hours of PRRT ending, consider prophylactic steroid taper. Regimens include methylprednisolone 6-day taper or 21-day dexamethasone taper. 

- ~~Timing of~~ SSAs (Octreotiede or Lanreotide) in Relation to Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate: 

- �Third bullet revised: ~~There are~~ _Due to_ theoretical concerns regarding the competition between SSAs and lutetium Lu 177 dotatate for SSTR binding _, clinical trials that have used PRRT and FDA guidance require/recommend withholding SSAs for at least 4 weeks prior to each PRRT cycle, however there is little evidence to support this practice. The Panel notes that it does not need to be strictly 28 days._ ~~Therefore, the following actions are recommended:~~

- Do not administer long-acting SSAs for 4 weeks prior to each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment. 

- Stop short-acting SSAs 24 hours before each lutetium LU 177 dotatate treatment. 

- SSAs (short- and long-acting) can be resumed 4–24 hours after each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment.

- New references added: 

- �Reference 11: Singh S, Halperin D, Myrehaug S, et al. [177Lu]Lu-DOTA-TATE plus long-acting octreotide versus high-dose long-acting octreotide for the treatment of newly diagnosed, advanced grade 2–3, well-differentiated, gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumours (NETTER-2): An openlabel, randomised, phase 3 study. Lancet 2024;403:2807-2817. 

- �Reference 13: Pelle E, Al-Toubah T, El-Haddad G, et al. Risk of bowel ischemia in patients with mesenteric neuroendocrine tumors after treatment with 177Lu-DOTATATE. Endocr Oncol 2025;5:e250033. 

- �Reference 14: Strosberg JR, Al-Toubah T, Pellè E, et al. Risk of bowel obstruction in patients with mesenteric or peritoneal disease receiving peptide receptor radionuclide therapy. J Nucl Med 2021;62:69-72. 

- �Reference 15: Bardasi C, Benatti S, Luppi G, et al. Carcinoid crisis: A misunderstood and unrecognized oncological emergency. Cancers (Basel) 2022;14:662.

- Carcinoid Syndrome, fifth bullet revised: ~~Octreotide should be available as needed during surgical procedures for patients with carcinoid syndrome who develop hemodynamic instability that could indicate carcinoid crisis. Doses of octreotide (100–500 mcg IV) can be used, potentially followed by IV infusion of octreotide (50–300 mcg/h).~~ ~~_P_~~ _rophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide administration should not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration to treat carcinoid crisis._ 

- NE-K 3 of 3 

- Insulin, Management, first bullet revised: Stabilize glucose levels with diet _(complex carbohydrates)_ and/or diazoxide or everolimus. 

**UPDATES**

**Neuroendocrine Tumors (NETs) of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2), Lung, and Thymus Clinical presentations:** 

- **Gastric (NET-1)** 

- **Duodenal (NET-2)** 

- **Appendix (NET-3)** 

- **Jejunal, ileal, colon (NET-4)** 

- **Rectal (NET-5)** 

**Well-Diferentiated, Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors (WDG3-1)** 

**Neuroendocrine Neoplasms of Unknown Primary (NUP-1) Extrapulmonary Poorly Diferentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm (MiNEN) (PDNEC-1)** 

- **Lung (NET-6)** 

- **Thymus (NET-7)** 

- **Locoregional advanced disease and/or distant metastases of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract (NET-9)** 

- **Locoregional unresectable lung/thymic NETs (NET-11)** 

- **Distant metastatic lung/thymic NETs (NET-12) • Multiple lung nodules or tumorlets and evidence of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia (DIPNECH) (NET-13)** 

- **Carcinoid syndrome (NET-14)**

**Clinical presentations:** 

- **Nonfunctioning pancreatic tumors (PanNET-1)** 

- **Gastrinoma (PanNET-3)** 

- **Insulinoma (PanNET-5)** 

- **Glucagonoma (PanNET-7)** 

- **VIPoma (PanNET-9)** 

- **Locoregional advanced disease and/or distant metastases (PanNET-12)** 

**Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Diferentiated Grade 1/2 NETs (SSTRNEG-1)** 

**Pheochromocytoma (PCC)/Paraganglioma (PGL) (PHEO-1)** 

**Adrenal Gland Tumors (AGT-1)[c]** 

- **Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia, Type 1 (MEN1) (MEN1-1) • Parathyroid** 

- **Pancreatic NETs (PanNETs)** 

- **Pituitary tumor** 

- **Lung/thymic NETs** 

**Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia, Type 2 (MEN2) (MEN2-1)** 

- **Medullary thyroid carcinoma (Also see NCCN Guidelines for Thyroid Carcinoma)** 

- **Parathyroid** 

- **PCC** 

**Merkel Cell Carcinoma (See NCCN Guidelines for Merkel Cell Carcinoma)**

b Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A). c Includes adrenocortical tumors and incidentalomas.

header: **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.**

content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

- b The minimum standard is to obtain at least two biopsies from the antrum (lesser and greater curvature), one biopsy from the incisura (angularis), and two biopsies from the gastric body (lesser and greater curvature). Dixon MF, et al. Am J Surg Pathol 1996;20:1161-1181. 

- c Serum gastrin can be falsely elevated with proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use. To confirm diagnosis, it should ideally be checked when fasting and off PPI for >1 week. However, PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms of gastrinoma and/or risks of complications. 

- d Elevated gastrin levels are usually suggestive of type 1 or type 2 tumors. 

- e For rare, >2 cm, type 1 gastric tumors, workup should include multiphasic CT or MRI of the abdomen. For metastatic disease, see NET-9. 

- f Type 3 gastric NETs (gNETs) are sporadic and unifocal. 

- g There is increasing evidence that patients who have been on long-term PPI therapy may be at an increased risk of developing gNETs, which appear to have a much lower propensity to metastasize than sporadic type 3 tumors. 

- h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- i SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. 

- j SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). 

- k Hirasawa T, et al. Dig Endosc 2021;33:408-417. 

- l Endoscopic resection should be reserved for small (<1 cm), superficial, low-grade tumors.

content:
**EVALUATION[a] ADDITIONAL SURVEILLANCE TREATMENT** 

**Tumor <1 cm Tumor ≥1 to ≤2 cm[r]** 

**No surveillance indicated Optional surveillance every 2–5 y based on clinicopathologic features** 

**Recommended: Tumor >2 cm • Multiphasic[h] or abdomen/pelvis CT Any tumor size with or MRI (NE-B) Appendix[p] Simple incomplete resection or Consider right NET-8 appendectomy[q] positive lymph nodes hemicolectomy[s] As appropriate: within the appendectomy • Consider SSTRspecimen (if available for PET/CT or SSTRanalysis) (NE-D) PET/MRI[i,j ] (NE-B) Metastatic disease NET-9** 

- a Principles of Pathology (NE-E). 

- h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- i SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. 

- j SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68GaDOTATOC). 

- p Some appendiceal NETs will have mixed histology, including elements of adenocarcinoma. Such tumors should be managed according to colon cancer guidelines. See NCCN Guidelines for Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers. 

- q See Staging (ST-3). Some NCCN Member Institutions consider right hemicolectomy for 1- to 2-cm tumors with poor prognostic features. See Discussion for details. 

- r Ahmed FA, et al. Surgery 2024;175:251-257; Nesti C, et al. Lancet Oncol 2023;24:187-194. 

s Greater than 12 lymph nodes should be retrieved.

**Locoregional Bowel resection(s) with regional NET-8 disease lymphadenectomy[u] (NE-D) Metastatic NET-9 disease**

- h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with intravenous (IV) contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- i SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. 

- j SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). t SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI should not be used to limit the extent of 

lymphadenectomy or small bowel resection as sensitivity may not be adequate. u Should include: 

- Manual palpation of the entire bowel, as synchronous tumors may be present. 

- Assess for proximity to or involvement of the superior mesenteric artery and vein. • Resection to root of mesentery.

Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. j SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). ff Primary + regional lymph node resection should be considered to reduce future obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, bleeding, or perforation. gg For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered. hh If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA. ii Treatment with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide will likely be of greatest benefit in patients with SSTR-positive tumors. jj In select cases, it may be appropriate to proceed to front-line systemic therapy or locoregional therapy prior to or concurrently with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide. **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** **NET-9** Version 1.2026, 04/21/2026 © 2026 National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ), All rights reserved. content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

**Asymptomatic, low tumor burden and low grade (typical carcinoid)** 

**Observe or Octreotide acetate LAR[gg,hh] or lanreotide[gg,hh] (if SSTR-positive and/or hormonal symptoms)** 

**Chest CT with contrast and multiphasic[h] abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI every 3–6 mo** 

**Lung/Thymus distant metastases Clinically significant tumor burden and low grade (typical carcinoid) or Evidence of disease progression or Intermediate grade (atypical carcinoid)[x] or Symptomatic disease** 

**Clinical trial (preferred) or Observation, in select patients[qq] or Systemic therapy (NE-G) or Locoregional therapy options • Liver-directed therapy for liverpredominant disease (NE-H)** 

**If progression on first-line therapy, consider changing to alternate first-line therapy[kk]** 

- h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- x Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 

gg For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered. 

hh If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA. 

- kk If clinically significant disease progression, treatment with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be discontinued for non-functional tumors and continued in patients with functional tumors; these regimens may be used in combination with any of the subsequent options. 

- ll For symptom control, consider addition of focal therapy (eg, endobronchial therapy, ablation). 

- pp NETs are highly heterogeneous and all elements need to be considered (eg, burden of disease, symptoms, histopathology, rate of growth) when determining the best course of treatment. 

- qq Observation can be considered in select patients with low burden of disease and favorable prognostic features.

e Observation can be considered for small (≤2 cm), low-grade, incidentally discovered, non-functional tumors. Decision based on estimated surgical risk, site of tumor, and patient comorbidities (Sadot E, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2016;23:1361-1370; Partelli S, et al. Br J Surg 2022;109:1186-1190; Heidsma CM, et al. Br J Surg 2021;108:888-891). Follow surveillance recommendations on PanNET-11. f Patients with germline mutations are treated based on any tumors of a concerning size. > g Consider belzutifan for resectable tumors in the setting of germline _VHL_ alteration. The decision to use belzutifan in small resectable tumors needs to be individualized. Data for the use of belzutifan for larger tumors, locally advanced unresectable tumors, and distant disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials are ongoing. - h Preoperative vaccination against encapsulated bacterium (ie, pneumococcus, haemophilus influenzae b, meningococcal group C), if considering surgery with possible splenectomy. See Principles of Surgical Management of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE-D 2 of 3). - i As appropriate, central pancreatectomy or spleen-preserving surgery can be considered. j PanNETs that are 1–2 cm have a small, but real risk of lymph node metastases. Therefore, lymph node resection should be considered.

**PanNET-11**| bb If clinically significant disease progression, treatment with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be discontinued for non-functional tumors and continued in patients with functional tumors; these regimens may be used in combination with any of the subsequent options. - b Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. - c SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. - cc Staged or synchronous resection when possible. When performing staged pancreatoduodenectomy and liver resection, consider hepatectomy prior to pancreatic resection in order to reduce risk of perihepatic sepsis. De Jong MC, et al. Ann Surg 2010;252:142-148. - d SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). > dd Use of belzutifan in the setting of germline _VHL_ alteration needs to be individualized (category 2B). Data for the use of belzutifan for larger tumors, locally advanced unresectable tumors, and distant disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials are ongoing. - m For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered. ee For patients with insulinoma, octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be used only if SSTR-based imaging is positive. If used, they should be used with caution in patients with insulinoma as they may transiently worsen hypoglycemia (see Discussion for details). - ff In select cases, it may be appropriate to proceed to front-line systemic therapy or locoregional therapy prior to or concurrently with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide. - n If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA.

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**

content:
a SSTR-negative refers to tumors that show insufficient uptake on SSTR-based imaging (lower than liver, Krenning score 0–2). 

- b SSTR-negative disease and mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease is more common with higher grade (G3) disease and atypical lung carcinoid. 

- c Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- d  In select cases, it may be appropriate to proceed to front-line systemic therapy or locoregional therapy prior to or concurrently with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide. 

- e Observation is an option for SSTR-negative, non-functional tumors with low disease burden. Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3–6 months) is recommended because SSTR-negative disease has a worse prognosis. Refardt J, et al. Endocr Relat Cancer 2020;27:615-624. 

- f Consider use of SSAs in patients with SSTR-negative disease and non-functional tumors. 

- g Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3–6 months) is recommended for patients with SSTR-negative disease who are treated with SSAs. 

- h PRRT can be considered for mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease but it might not work as well as for homogenous SSRT-positive disease. Treatment may also need to incorporate strategies to address the SSTR-negative disease. See Principles of Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy with Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate (NE-J). 

- i For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered. 

- j If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA. 

**SSTRNEG-1A**

content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

- **Multiphasic[b] abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with contrast (NE-B)** 

- **Chest CT as clinically indicated** 

- **Pathology review** 

- **SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI[c,d ] (NE-B)**

- **Well• FDG-PET/CT[e]** 

- **differentiated Grade 3 NETs** 

   - **Biochemical evaluation as clinically indicated (NE-A)** 

   - **Consider molecular profiling of tumor tissue[f] • Consider assessment of p53, Rb, p16 by histopathologic analysis or molecular profiling if uncertain about differentiation** 

   - **• Genetic counseling and testing for inherited genetic syndromes (only for duodenal NET or PanNET) (NE-F)** 

**Locoregional WDG3-2 (Resectable)** 

**Favorable biology (eg, relatively low Ki-67[g] [<55%], slow growing, WDG3-3 positive SSTR-based PET imaging) Locally advanced/ Metastatic** 

**Unfavorable biology (eg, relatively high Ki-67[g ] [≥55%], faster growing, WDG3-4 negative SSTR-based PET imaging)**

- b Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- c SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following administration of SSAs. 

- d SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). 

e Consider both FDG-PET and DOTATATE-PET if considering PRRT. 

- f Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 

g There are limitations in terms of the data for what the appropriate cutoff should be, as well as variability/heterogeneity of Ki-67 in a given tumor and over time in serial biopsies. The clinical course can be heterogeneous and treatment considerations need to account for both pathologic and clinical features. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.**

**MANAGEMENT OF LOCALLY ADVANCED/METASTATIC DISEASE: UNFAVORABLE BIOLOGY**
content:
**TREATMENT** 

**SURVEILLANCE** 

**Locally advanced/Metastatic disease: Unfavorable biology (relatively high Ki-67 [≥55%],[g] rapid growth rate, FDG-avid tumors, negative SSTR-based PET imaging)**

**or Locoregional therapy options** 

- **Consider RT (NE-I) ± concurrent fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy for locally advanced unresectable disease** 

- **Consider addition of liver-directed therapy[m] (NE-H)** 

- **Palliative RT for oligometastatic disease and/or symptomatic metastases (excluding mesenteric masses)[j] (NE-I)** 

**or Resection may play a role in select cases (NE-D)** 

**Every 8–12 wk (depending on tumor biology)** 

- **H&P** 

- **Multiphasic[b] abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with contrast (NE-B)** 

- **Chest CT (± contrast) as clinically indicated** 

- **FDG-PET/CT as clinically indicated (NE-B)** 

- **Biochemical markers as clinically indicated (NE-A)** 

b Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. 

- g There are limitations in terms of the data for what the appropriate cutoff should be, as well as variability/heterogeneity of Ki-67 in a given tumor and over time in serial biopsies. The clinical course can be heterogeneous and treatment considerations need to account for both pathologic and clinical features. 

- j Krug S, et al. BMC 2019;19:362. 

m Consider liver-directed therapy in selected cases with residual liver-predominant disease after systemic therapy. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.**

header: a Eads JR, et al. Endocr Relat Cancer 2023;30:e220206.
content:
- b Sorbye H, et al. J Neuroendocrinol 2023;35:e13249. 

- c This page is for poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma (PDNEC) and not high-grade NET. Not all high-grade (Ki-67 >20%) neuroendocrine neoplasms are poorly differentiated. See WDG3-1. 

- d PDNECs are often associated with non-neuroendocrine components such as adeno or squamous cell carcinoma. Management of these tumors is controversial. Often, chemotherapy regimens for non-neuroendocrine components may be considered. 

- e SSTR-based imaging (SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI) is not part of the routine evaluation of PDNECs. 

f Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. g Tumor/somatic molecular profiling is recommended for patients with locoregional unresectable/ metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 

h Combination of immune checkpoint inhibitors + chemotherapy is investigational for all patients with extrapulmonary PDNECs. 

i Earlier, if symptoms. 

j NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship.

**• Genetic counseling and testing for inherited genetic syndromes(NE-F)**
**As appropriate, if metastatic or multifocal disease suspectedf (NE-B):**
**• Multiphasicg abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI**

**(SPECT)/CTj**
**• Consider molecular profling of tumor tissuek**|| |---|---| ||| a Principles of Pathology (NE-E). b Consider medical alert ID for hormonally secreting PCCs and PGLs in situ or metastatic disease. c Review concurrent medication(s) for those that may interfere with plasma metanephrines evaluation. Elevations that are 3 times above the upper limit of normal are diagnostic. d For cervical PGL, measuring serum and/or 24-hour urine fractionated catecholamines (for dopamine) or methoxytyramine (if available) may be appropriate. Methoxytyramine is helpful as the metabolite of dopamine in cervical tumors but it is not commercially available in many places. e Both catecholamines and metanephrines/normetanephrines can represent false-positive results. Some investigators support the use of fractionated urinary and/or plasma catecholamines as a way to differentiate false-positive results and to differentiate epinephrine-secreting PCC. f Data on the role of functional imaging in PCC/PGL are evolving and the preferred method remains unclear. g Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases. h SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. i SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC). j MIBG scans are less sensitive than FDG-PET and 68Ga-DOTATATE for metastatic and multifocal PCCs/PGLs (in patients with von Hippel Lindau [VHL] and succinate dehydrogenase [SDH] syndromes but not patients with MEN1 or NF1 syndromes or some patients with sporadic PCC). SPECT/CT imaging of involved sites is recommended. Obtain MIBG scan if considering treatment with I131-MIBG. Timmers HJLM, et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2012;104:700-708. k Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible.

Cardiac arrhythmias can be managed with esmolol or lidocaine. Hypoglycemia and hypotension can be seen postoperatively. Treat with IV fluids for volume expansion and include dextrose for glucose levels. - n Alpha 1 selective receptor blockers include terazosin, doxazosin, and prazosin, and non-selective receptor blockers include phenoxybenzamine. Therapy for 7–14 days is recommended prior to surgical therapy. Nonselective alpha blockade phentolamine (IV) can be used intraoperatively. Doxazosin has a longer half-life and is oftentimes more available than some of the other agents. - Alpha blockade is necessary treatment for all hormonally secreting PCCs and PGLs regardless of clinical symptoms. If additional treatments are planned, patients may need medication adjustments. After alpha blockade, if additional blood pressure support is needed, the addition of dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers can be used. This is not recommended as monotherapy unless the patient cannot tolerate alpha blockade. Metyrosine can also be used in addition to alpha blockade to stabilize blood pressure. Beta blockade can be added to alpha blockade for tachycardia. B1 selective blockers or nonselective beta blockers can be used. Combination beta/alpha blockers are not recommended. - p Fishbein L, et al. Pancreas 2021;50:469-493.

header: **Patients with MEN1 should be screened for all of the following tumor types, starting at 8–15 years of age:[1-6]**
content:
**If calcium rises:[c]** 

- **PTH**

- **Calcium and albumin annually** 

- **25-OH vitamin D** 

- **Alkaline phosphatase** 

- **Re-image with neck ultrasound and/or parathyroid sestamibi with SPECT scan (SPECT-CT preferred) or 4D-CT[b,d ] (NE-B)**

- **Follow previously elevated serum hormones or as symptoms indicate** 

- **Consider abdomen/pelvis MRI[b] (preferred) or CT with contrast every 1–3 y** 

**See appropriate sporadic PanNET workup and treatment (PanNET-1 through PanNET-10)** 

- **Consider serial EUS**

- **Pituitary MRI with contrast every 3–5 y** 

- **Prolactin, insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), and other previously abnormal pituitary hormones every 3–5 y or as symptoms indicate**

- **Consider chest CT or MRI[b] with contrast every 1–3 y** 

**See appropriate workup and treatment for lung (NET-6) or thymic (NET-7) NETs**

- **Angiomas** 

- **Adrenal tumors** 

- **Collagenomas** 

- **Lipomas** 

- **Angiofibromas** 

a Consider referral to an endocrinologist. 

b For prolonged surveillance, studies without radiation are preferred. 

- c Further studies should be done, especially in patients with suspected MEN1, when the serum calcium is ≥10 mg/dL since normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism is noted especially as an early sign of the disease. Hyperparathyroidism evaluations should include consideration of other secondary causes of hypercalcemia. 

d Preference of scan will depend on institutional practice/protocol. Sestamibi may not be as sensitive since often the patient has hyperplasia. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** 

**References on MEN1-4**

content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

header: **TREATMENT OF PanNETs SPECIFIC TO PATIENTS WITH MEN1[1,9,10]**
content:
- **In general, surgical treatment of patients with MEN1 is similar to those with sporadic tumors. Refer to the relevant site-specific** 

- **recommendations for PanNETs earlier in these guidelines (PanNET-1 through PanNET-10).** 

- **However, one notable exception is the multifocality of pancreatoduodenal NETs in patients with MEN1. The role of surgery remains controversial in patients with multifocal tumors.** 

- **Decision to resect a pancreatic or duodenal NET in the setting of multifocal disease is complex. If surgery is performed to resect hormonally functional tumor(s), attempts should be made to preoperatively localize the site of the functional tumor. Surgical resection can be considered in the following scenarios:** 

- **Symptomatic functional tumors refractory to medical management** 

- **Tumor >2 cm in size** 

- **Tumor with relatively rapid rate of growth over 6–12 months** 

- **MEN1-associated metastatic PanNETs are often slower growing than metastatic sporadic tumors. Observation can be considered for nonfunctioning indolent tumors.** 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** 

**References on MEN1-4** 

**MEN1-3**

**SURVEILLANCE**| |---|---| a Preference of scan will depend on institutional practice/protocol. Sestamibi scan may not be as sensitive as other imaging options since often the patient has hyperplasia. b Evaluation of PCC should be done before the administration of any anesthetic or invasive procedure. c More likely to be multifocal. d For synchronous bilateral PCCs, a bilateral adrenalectomy is recommended. e Consider medical alert ID for hormonally secreting PCCs in situ or metastatic disease. f Review concurrent medication(s) for those that may interfere with plasma metanephrines evaluation. Elevations that are 3 times above the upper limit of normal are diagnostic. g Both catecholamines and metanephrines/normetanephrines can represent false-positive results. Some investigators support the use of fractionated urinary and/or plasma catecholamines as a way to differentiate false-positive results and to differentiate epinephrine-secreting PCC. h For the treatment of synchronous tumors, surgical resection of PCC should take priority over thyroidectomy for medullary thyroid carcinoma. i Subtotal parathyroidectomy is recommended when all the parathyroid glands are abnormal. Some thyroid surgeons recommend total parathyroidectomy with parathyroid autotransplantation, but others believe the risk of hypoparathyroidism (~6%) is too high to warrant this procedure.

- Some NETs can secrete specific neuroendocrine hormones. Hormonal workup should be guided by the presence of symptoms of the excess hormone. Screening for hormones in individuals with asymptomatic disease is not routinely required. - �Patients with functional tumors have clinical symptoms related to tumor-associated hormone excess. • PPIs, other drugs, some medical conditions, and certain foods are known to cause false elevations in serum gastrin and chromogranin A. To confirm diagnosis, serum gastrin should ideally be checked when fasting and off PPI for >1 week. However, PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms of gastrinoma and/ or risks of complications. - If MEN2 is suspected, patients should be evaluated for PCC/PGL prior to any procedures.[8] **==> picture [747 x 19] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

- b Some drugs may interfere with testing results, including: acetaminophen, labetalol, sotalol, α-methyldopa, tricyclic antidepressants, buspirone, phenoxybenzamine, monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), sympathomimetics, cocaine, sulfasalazine, and levodopa. 

- c Methoxytyramine is helpful as the metabolite of dopamine in cervical tumors but it is not commercially available in many places. 

- d For additional information on biochemical testing for hypercortisolemia (± Cushing syndrome), refer to the Endocrine Society's Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Treatment of Cushing's Syndrome: Fleseriu M, Auchus R, Bancos I, et al. Consensus on diagnosis and management of Cushing's disease: a guideline update. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2021;9:847-875. 

- e Petrosal vein sampling can be considered to differentiate adrenal from pituitary and ectopic causes. Petrosal sinus sampling only if ACTH-dependent hypercortisolemia (± Cushing syndrome). 

- f Oral or IV salt loading and measurement of urine or plasma aldosterone to test for adequate suppression.

header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**
content: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion

- Cross-sectional imaging should include the primary site of disease. Either CT or MRI is appropriate. 

- The liver is a common metastatic site for NET, and these metastatic lesions are often hypervascular. Multiphase (dual-phase) contrast-enhanced (arterial and portal venous phase) CT is important because NETs frequently enhance in the arterial phase. SSTR-PET/CT should be interpreted in combination with dual-phase CT or MRI to identify both SSTR(+) and SSTR(-) sites of disease. PET/CT and PET/MRI scanners are capable of providing full diagnostic multiphase contrast-enhanced CT and MRI scans. This provides the most accurate correlation of anatomic sites and receptor status. When possible, SSTR-PET/CT and SSTR-PET/MRI should be performed concurrently with diagnostic multiphase CT or MRI. Consider same imaging modality and contrast agent (either multiphasic CT or MRI) for follow-up studies for consistent imaging interpretation. 

- Without a known tumor or specific clinical concern, imaging should include chest CT ± contrast per institutional standards and imaging of the brain is generally not required for well-differentiated NET. 

- • For metastatic well-differentiated NET, anatomic imaging should generally be performed every 12 weeks–12 months based on clinical or pathologic signs of aggressiveness. 

- Consider MRI over CT to minimize radiation risk.

- Evaluation with SSTR imaging to assess receptor status and distant disease is appropriate. This is especially important for determining whether a patient may benefit from SSTR-directed therapy. 

- �SSTR-based imaging options include SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI, or octreotide SPECT/CT (only if SSTR-PET is not available). 

- �Examples of appropriate SSTR-PET tracers: 68Ga-DOTATATE, 68Ga-DOTATOC, or 64Cu-DOTATATE. 

- �SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions is greater than liver. 

- For qualitative lesion assessment, Modified Krenning score can be used. Krenning score is based on the lesion with the highest SSTR uptake: 

   - 0, no uptake 

   - 1, very low uptake 

   - 2, uptake ≤ liver 

   - 3, uptake > liver 

   - 4, uptake > spleen 

- Whenever possible, SSTR-PET/CT should be performed in combination with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI (dual-phase hepatic CT or MRI imaging preferred) to minimize the total number of imaging studies. The contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is vital to identify lesions that are SSTR-negative as well as those that are SSTR-positive. 

- Octreotide SPECT/CT is much less sensitive for defining SSTR-positive disease than SSTR-PET/CT, and typically cannot be done in combination with multiphase CT or MRI. Therefore, SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI is preferred. 

- FDG-PET should be considered in select cases where G2 or higher NETs or NECs is documented. 

- As with SSTR-PET/CT, combining FDG-PET with dual-phase liver CT or MRI is preferred. 

- Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET, even for lower grade tumors, and a combined score of FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives more prognostic value, outperforms pathologic grading, and may identify more active disease.[1,2] 

a Data on the role of functional imaging in PCC/PGL is evolving and the preferred method remains unclear. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** 

**References**

- After potentially curative surgery, surveillance is recommended for at least 10 years for most patients. In certain cases, surveillance may be extended beyond 10 years based on risk factors such as age and risk of recurrence. However, data are limited on the optimal surveillance schedule beyond 10 years.

header: **- Transthoracic Echocardiogram to Assess for Carcinoid (NET Related) Heart Disease[3,4]**
content:
- Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is important for the evaluation of CHD and should include morphologic evaluation of the valves (especially tricuspid and pulmonary), as well as assessment of the right heart size and function. When valve disease is present, an agitated saline injection should be performed to determine whether there is an atrial level shunt. 

- Post valve replacement, patients should be followed with serial echocardiograms according to institutional practice. If valves are abnormal by echocardiogram, consider the potential for clots requiring resumption of anticoagulants versus recurrent CHD.

header: **Repeat TTE Recommendations for Patients with NETs:**
content:
|**Repeat TTE Recommendations for Patients with NETs:**|**Repeat TTE Recommendations for Patients with NETs:**| |---|---| |**Without known CHD**|• Symptoms of dyspnea, fatigue, edema, or ascites
• Physical exam fndings including elevated jugular venous pressure, edema, or ascites
• Prior to planned bowel/liver resection
• Reassessment every1–3years| |**Known CHD with or**
**without prior valve**
**surgery**|• Decision-making in conjunction with a multidisciplinary care team
• Annual reassessment of all valves (native ± prosthetic) in conjunction with specialty cardiology consultation
• New symptoms of dyspnea, fatigue, edema, or ascites
• Change in physical exam including elevated jugular venous pressure, edema, or ascites
• Prior to planned bowel/liver resection| **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** **References** **NE-B 2 OF 5** Version 1.2026, 04/21/2026 © 2026 National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ), All rights reserved. header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 1.2026 Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors**

content:
- Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis should be considered as the first step in patients with biochemically confirmed PCC/PGL. If this initial imaging is negative, CT or MRI of the chest and dedicated MRI of the neck should be considered. 

- Consider MRI over CT to minimize radiation risk, especially in children. 

- Depending upon the genetic mutations, being symptomatic and non-symptomatic, follow up with contrast-enhanced MRI of neck, thorax, abdomen, and pelvis is recommended.

- SSTR imaging, FDG PET, as well as iodine-123 metaiodobenzylguanidine (I-123 MIBG) scintigraphy. 

- Evaluation with SSTR imaging to assess receptor status and distant disease is appropriate. SSTR imaging is particularly sensitive in patients with metastatic disease, with SDHx pathogenic variants, as well as with head and neck PCC/PGL. 

- SSTR-based imaging options include SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI, or examples of appropriate SSTR-PET tracers: 68Ga-DOTATATE, 68GaDOTATOC, or 64Cu-DOTATATE. 

- SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions > liver. 

- Whenever possible, SSTR-PET should be performed in combination with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI to minimize the total number of imaging studies. The contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is vital to identify lesions that are SSTR-negative as well as those that are SSTR-positive. 

- FDG-PET may be considered if SSTR imaging is not available. Relatively decreased sensitivity due to brown fat activation, nonspecific uptake in inflammation, and uptake in other tumors. However, good sensitivity in PCC/PGL, particularly in patients harbouring germline pathogenic variants in cluster 1 genes, and in patients with metastatic PCC/PGL. 

- 123I MIBG has lower sensitivity as a staging test in patients with cluster 1 PCC/PGL compared with patients with cluster 2 or sporadic PCC/PGL. Considering that most children with PCC/PGL harbour cluster 1 gene variants, I-123 MIBG should be typically reserved for patients with metastatic disease in whom radiolabelled iodine-131 metaiodobenzylguanidine (I-131 MIBG) therapy is being considered.

- For metastatic evaluation, consider contrast-enhanced CT chest, contrast-enhanced MRI brain.

- FDG-PET is an adjunct modality for local staging, recurrence, evaluation for metastatic disease, and treatment response. 

- Bone scintigraphy may be considered for evaluation of metastatic bone disease.
